description: Anaximander was Thales' student and immediate successor in the Milesian school. He inherited the fundamental project — finding the arche through natural rather than mythological explanation — but gave a philosophical argument against his teacher's specific answer. The move from 'water' to 'the Apeiron' is the move from assertion to argument, from a concrete answer to an abstract one. Anaximander thus both extends and implicitly criticises Thales, demonstrating that the philosophical tradition begins with internal critique from its very first generation.
source: Aristotle, Physics 203b; Simplicius, Commentary on Aristotle's Physics
